3: In Germany in 2012, the number of anaerobic digestion (AD) plants that process agricultural raw materials increased to 7874. During the same period, their installed power output was 3384 MWel (Fachverband Biogas e.V.) and their total gross electricity production was 27,239 GWh/a (EUROBSERV'ER, 2014). These translate into 0.43 MWe1/AD unit and 3.46 GWh/AD unit per year, respectively. Meanwhile, in the United Kingdom (UK), the second largest European Union (EU) biogas market, primary energy pro- duction from biogas was based mainly on landfill gas and sewage sludge gas (EUROBSERV'ER, 2012). The number of farm-scale biogas plants in the UK reached 78 in 2012 (Committee on Climate Change, 2013), with an installed electrical capacity of 110 MW (Department of Energy and Climate Change, 2014). In the USA, the number of farm-scale biogas plants was 193, with an installed electrical ca- pacity of 160.1 MW; however, only 3% of these AD plants used co-
fermentation; the rest were based on manure from animal hus- bandry (AgSTAR US EPA, 2014). The country specific biogas pro- duction patterns are depicted in Table 1 below.

These numbers show that other countries undoubtedly lag behind Germany in biogas production. Nonetheless, it is worth noting that the majority of German AD plants produce biogas from arable-land-based biomass, primarily maize silage. The by- products of animal husbandry are only partially used for power generation. As a comparison, energy crops used as raw materials for biogas plants were cultivated on 800,000 ha out of 11.874 million hectares of agricultural land in Germany (Statistisches Bundesamt, 2013). To manage valuable trade-offs that are worth paying, it is necessary to supply policy developers with accurate numbers with regard to environmental performance for these technologies.

When potential environmental policy goals are set to increase the renewable energy production on the basis of AD plants, possible raw material sources and utilisation chains must be examined, i.e. energy crop cultivation or waste recycling. The potential environ- mental effects should also be considered, and the direction of further development determined on the basis of accurately

calculated figures. Previous studies (Berglund, 2006; Finnveden et al., 2005; Jury et al., 2010; Poeschl et al., 2010, 2012; Scholwin, 2006) neglected the GHG emissions related to the construction of an AD plant during the full life cycle (assumed to be less than 1%, which is possible in some cases). Several calculations have been made to compare the CO2 emissions of biogas plants, and these indicated a positive CO2 balance (Bacenetti et al., 2013; Bachmaier et al., 2010; Bachmaier, 2012; Bohn et al., 2007; Börjesson and Berglund, 2006; Jury et al., 2010; Scholwin, 2006) when produc- ing 1 kWh electricity; however, these values have not been compared with other environmental parameters, such as acidifi- cation or eutrophication. The centralised or distributed infrastruc- ture of AD plants has already been investigated (Berglund, 2006; Patterson et al., 2011; Poeschl et al., 2010) and showed marginal extra-environmental effects if the substrates were transported over longer distances. Thus, the positive economic effects of scale-up with bigger fermentation capacities can be realised while keeping environmental burdens low.

The present study aimed to examine the environmental burden of high-tech industrialised, but farm-scale AD plants, and the effect of utilising various agricultural raw materials on the environmental performance of the examined biogas plants. To analyse the envi- ronmental effects of possible substrate usage strategies in agricul- ture, detailed data on the erection and operation of three AD plants were collected and analysed.

8: To achieve the previously set goal, LCA, based on the EN ISO 14044:2006 standard (International Organization for Standardization, 2006), was performed with the GaBi 6 life cycle analysis software (PE International AG) using the Ecoinvent 2.2 database (Swiss Centre for Life Cycle Inventories, 2010) accompa- nied by desk research and data collected on-site.
9: EN ISO 14044:2006 requires the definition of the product sys- tem and the functional unit of LCA. In the present study, the product system inspected was AD of energy crops and/or agricultural wastes from animal husbandry in small-scale decentralised AD plants. The generated biogas was used in a cogeneration unit to produce heat and electricity.

Here, the end product of the examined process, using a cradle- to-gate approach, is the electricity exported (kWh) into the grid. It is also its functional unit (FU), to which all the relevant envi- ronmental effects are referred.

The first step to define the life cycle's environmental effects was to determine the life cycle's boundaries as depicted in Fig. 1 and its time span (International Organization for Standardization, 2006;

Klöpffer and Grahl, 2009). In this case, a 'cradle-to-gate' life cycle was analysed, just as in many recent studies (Bacenetti et al., 2013; Lijó et al., 2014b); this means that all of the materials and energy flows necessary for the establishment and operation of the AD plant, and the land use requirement, were considered with all their upstream material and energy flows up to the produced energy unit. Therefore, the system boundary of the examined life cycle starts with the construction of the AD plant comprising up-flows of all materials and energy inputs during the operation and includes the cultivation of necessary feedstock. The process ends when biogas is transformed to electricity (FU) at the gas engine generator.

The input side of the inspected product system has construction-related and feedstock-related input flows. Among these flows, construction flows and energy-crop-based feedstock are considered with their full up-flow streams, according to the cradle-to-gate approach. The 'gate-to-gate' life cycle was only considered with animal-husbandrv-based feedstock, such as manure and slurry, because here only direct manure emissions were calculated for the AD plant, whereas upstream flows, such as material and energy flows of animal breeding and its necessary crop cultivation, were not. This is because these environmental effects are mainly due to primary products, such as meat or milk, and only partially burden side products or wastes, such as manure and slurry. This corresponds to the allocation procedure of the Ecoinvent 2.2 AD LCA descriptions (Jungbluth et al., 2007). Most biogas LCA studies entirely neglect these whole upstream flows of manure (Bachmaier et al., 2010; Bachmaier, 2012; Berglund, 2006) or use a replacement approach (Bacenetti et al., 2013; Lijó et al., 2014b). However, because some elementary flows (mainly emis- sions from temporary storage and manipulation) of manure emerge during the utilisation process, we decided to consider them. Transport expenditures of the substrate, if not generated on site, were taken into account as environmental burdens.

To make comparisons possible, the observed AD plants were selected such that the produced biogas quantity could be the same every year, i.e. the fermentation size of plants was adjusted to the produced electricity on a yearly basis. Thus, all the examined biogas plants were designed with an identical electricity power capacity (600 kW). However, the AD plants operated on different agricul- tural raw-material flows. The life span of an AD plant was deter- mined to be 20 years, and other equipment, such as pumps, stirrers, solid material feeding systems, and combined heat and power (CHP) with a shorter life span, were consequently considered. This equipment was accounted for at least twice in the life cycle in- ventory analyses to model the necessary replacements.

However, because some elementary flows (mainly emis- sions from temporary storage and manipulation) of manure emerge during the utilisation process, we decided to consider them. Transport expenditures of the substrate, if not generated on site, were taken into account as environmental burdens.

To make comparisons possible, the observed AD plants were selected such that the produced biogas quantity could be the same every year, i.e. the fermentation size of plants was adjusted to the produced electricity on a yearly basis. Thus, all the examined biogas plants were designed with an identical electricity power capacity (600 kW). However, the AD plants operated on different agricul- tural raw-material flows. The life span of an AD plant was deter- mined to be 20 years, and other equipment, such as pumps, stirrers, solid material feeding systems, and combined heat and power (CHP) with a shorter life span, were consequently considered. This equipment was accounted for at least twice in the life cycle in- ventory analyses to model the necessary replacements.
11: EN ISO 14044:2006 requires the definition of the product sys- tem and the functional unit of LCA. In the present study, the product system inspected was AD of energy crops and/or agricultural wastes from animal husbandry in small-scale decentralised AD plants. The generated biogas was used in a cogeneration unit to produce heat and electricity.

Here, the end product of the examined process, using a cradle- to-gate approach, is the electricity exported (kWh) into the grid. It is also its functional unit (FU), to which all the relevant envi- ronmental effects are referred.

The first step to define the life cycle's environmental effects was to determine the life cycle's boundaries as depicted in Fig. 1 and its time span (International Organization for Standardization, 2006;

Klöpffer and Grahl, 2009). In this case, a 'cradle-to-gate' life cycle was analysed, just as in many recent studies (Bacenetti et al., 2013; Lijó et al., 2014b); this means that all of the materials and energy flows necessary for the establishment and operation of the AD plant, and the land use requirement, were considered with all their upstream material and energy flows up to the produced energy unit. Therefore, the system boundary of the examined life cycle starts with the construction of the AD plant comprising up-flows of all materials and energy inputs during the operation and includes the cultivation of necessary feedstock. The process ends when biogas is transformed to electricity (FU) at the gas engine generator.

The input side of the inspected product system has construction-related and feedstock-related input flows. Among these flows, construction flows and energy-crop-based feedstock are considered with their full up-flow streams, according to the cradle-to-gate approach. The 'gate-to-gate' life cycle was only considered with animal-husbandrv-based feedstock, such as manure and slurry, because here only direct manure emissions were calculated for the AD plant, whereas upstream flows, such as material and energy flows of animal breeding and its necessary crop cultivation, were not. This is because these environmental effects are mainly due to primary products, such as meat or milk, and only partially burden side products or wastes, such as manure and slurry. This corresponds to the allocation procedure of the Ecoinvent 2.2 AD LCA descriptions (Jungbluth et al., 2007). 13: As a next step, the list of material and energy flows entering and leaving the life cycle was compiled. This is life cycle inventory analysis (LCI), which contains all the material and energy flows with an environmental effect, either absorbed from the environ- ment or injected into it. These are the so-called elementary flows. In Fig. 1, the elementary flows are indicated by the exiting arrows from the field of production on both the input and output sides. The material and energy flows inside the system serve only as system element interconnections, also known as tracked flows (Frischknecht and Jungbluth, 2007; Klöpffer and Grahl, 2009; Sára, 2010).
In the present study, three types of AD plant were examined. Their structures and characteristics are summarised in Table 2. The three plants were as follows.

<list>1. ADP1: Exclusively crop-based biogas production. 
2. ADP2: Crop-, slurry- and manure-based biogas production.</list>

14: <header>3. ADP3: Slurry- and manure-based biogas production. </header>

A modular structure was applied for the size design (adequate hydraulic retention time and digestate storage capacity) of the three different plant types. As shown in Table 2, the AD plants have the same tank unit volume, but different numbers of tanks. The waste-utilising third plant has the largest total fermentation vol- ume. Because it experienced the largest digested material flow, this plant also has to have the largest digestate storage tank.

Each AD plant was assumed to be a wet, two-stage, continuously fed and stirred process operating at a mesophilic temperature (40 °C). The hydraulic retention time (HRT) depends on the basic substrate (Wellinger et al., 2013). HRT is a minimum of 90 days if energy crops are even partially digested and 40 days if only animal by-products are fermented. The digestate storage capacity was calculated to be enough for 180 days. Thus, the first AD plant has the highest hydraulic retention time because energy crop materials need more time to be decomposed.

No pasteurisation of the digestate was assumed because, ac- cording to European laws (The European Parliament and Council, 2002), it is not necessary for digestate to be pasteurised when the digested substrate is utilised as an organic agricultural fertiliser.

17: The approach of the present study is somewhat different to that of previous assessments (Bacenetti et al., 2013; Bachmaier et al., 2010; Bachmaier, 2012; Berglund, 2006; Chevalier and Meunier, 2005; Djatkov et al., 2012; Hartmann, 2006; Jury et al., 2010; Lijó et al., 2014b; Mezzullo et al., 2013; Poeschl et al., 2012; Rehl and Müller, 2011; Tufvesson et al., 2013) because the effects of pro- cessed raw materials are partly considered and calculated into the burden of the biogas plant, irrespective of them being primary or secondary by-products. Thus, the full life cycle of an agricultural crop (Knudsen et al., 2014) is considered at the AD plant that pro- cesses energy crops. If animal manure is processed, the direct emissions of manure and slurry are occurring during the AD plant's operation are taken into account. The emissions of manure from animal husbandry have been studied previously (Amon et al., 2006; Dämmgen, 2009; European Environmental Agency, 2007; Lijó et al., 2014a; OECD, 2001; Perälä et al., 2006; Prapaspongsa et al., 2010; Rehl and Müller, 2011). The resulting data have been considered for the emission calculation of untreated manure or manure that was fermented by AD.

The direct emissions from manure management included in the present study are as follows (see Table 3 for specific figures): manure extraction from buildings or yards, and temporary storage and manure application on the basis of Western-European data sources (European Environmental Agency, 2007; OECD, 2001).
Therefore, when energy crops are used the full upstream flow is applied to the plant (cradle-to-gate life span) (Bacenetti et al., 2013;
Christensen et al., 2009; Hedegaard et al., 2008; Lijó et al., 2014b; Turconi et al., 2013); however, when manure is fermented, only the gate-to-gate cycle is applied.

The research of Perälä et al. (2006) showed that N20 emission is identical for the digested substrate and the untreated slurry. Amon et al. (2006) proved the same for NH3 and for other emissions. Thus, because animal manure digested in a biogas plant has the same emission values as untreated manure spread on arable land, only methane emission reduction was calculated. Methane emission was reduced by the proportion of biogas produced by the micro- biological degradation of volatile solids. These parameters have been considered in our calculations and refer to 1 m³ of the digested substrate.

The amount of material flows entering the air by flue gas when the gas engine is operated was calculated on the basis of the technical description of a 625-kW capacity gas engine (General Electric Jenbacher, 2010).

When determining GHG production during plant cultivation, the Ecoinvent 2.2 database was used for values of the cradle-to-gate LCA. In calculating the CO2 emission equivalent, the following modification factors were ignored.

<list>1. GHG reduction when surplus heat from the biogas engine is utilised in cogeneration and the previous fossil-based heat production is replaced. This is because heat utilisation happens very seldom at biogas plants. 
2. CO2 emission of 1 kWh electricity from the national energy mix. This is because the biogas plant is assumed to be self-sufficient in electricity consumption.</list>

21: When the technology calculations of the AD plants were pre- pared, construction and operation experiences as primary data directly obtained from two AD plants were used where agricultural raw materials (manure and energy crop) are processed. To deter- mine the exact amount of material flows for the construction of new AD plants, the material definitions of the construction plans as well as data of equipment and material suppliers were considered.

The main construction materials were assigned to each FU of the AD plant and were calculated separately. The Ecoinvent 2.2 data- base was used for the environmental impacts of these materials. The main units of the AD plant and these materials were taken into consideration during LCI: fermentation tank bases, hard standing/ roadways (gravel sub-base in three layers, reinforced concrete); solid material feeding system (steel); pumping station (concrete basement, galvanised steel frame, aluminium cladding) with screw pump; reception tank (reinforced concrete); fermentation tank (reinforced concrete, mineral-wool insulation, galvanised steel cladding, polyethylene (PE) and polyvinylchloride (PVC) gas storage membranes); digestate storage tank (gravel sub-base, reinforced concrete base, steel tank wall, one-layer polyethylene membrane); gas system (aboveground stainless steel, underground poly- ethylene); substrate piping (polyethylene); control panel; and ca- bling (copper cables with polyethylene cover). For all AD plants, it was assumed that the topsoil to a 1-m depth was excavated under the tanks and reused later on-site. The necessary transport dis- tances of the construction were separately assumed for each ma- terial flow. These were as follows: 15 km for concrete; 170 km for crushed gravel; 25 km for other steel and plastic products; 650 km for steel tank wall components; 790 km for PVC and PE mem- branes; and 635 km for main components (CHP, stirrers, control panel, cables). The total amounts are given in Table 4.
The necessary operational energy and material quantities of already-operating 600 kWel biogas plants were measured on-site,

and their figures used for calculating the parasitic electricity de- mand (PED) and heat consumptions of the model AD plants. These material flows also included lubrication oils and grease for the full lifetime of the AD plants. A 600-kW electrical capacity gas engine was selected to convert biogas into electricity, of which official technical data delivered by the constructors were used (General Electric Jenbacher, 2010) when LCI and material flow in operation were determined. Key parame- ters of the CHP unit are the electrical conversion efficiency (40.4%) and the thermal conversion efficiency (42.4%). During LCA, the size of the area used for AD plant establishment also has to be considered. National regulations usually allow an occupancy rate of a maximum 30% (Monson, 2007). Thus, the actual sealed surface with infrastructure was enlarged by an extra 70%.

The necessary transport dis- tances of the construction were separately assumed for each ma- terial flow. These were as follows: 15 km for concrete; 170 km for crushed gravel; 25 km for other steel and plastic products; 650 km for steel tank wall components; 790 km for PVC and PE mem- branes; and 635 km for main components (CHP, stirrers, control panel, cables). The total amounts are given in Table 4.
The necessary operational energy and material quantities of already-operating 600 kWel biogas plants were measured on-site,

and their figures used for calculating the parasitic electricity de- mand (PED) and heat consumptions of the model AD plants. These material flows also included lubrication oils and grease for the full lifetime of the AD plants. A 600-kW electrical capacity gas engine was selected to convert biogas into electricity, of which official technical data delivered by the constructors were used (General Electric Jenbacher, 2010) when LCI and material flow in operation were determined. Key parame- ters of the CHP unit are the electrical conversion efficiency (40.4%) and the thermal conversion efficiency (42.4%). During LCA, the size of the area used for AD plant establishment also has to be considered. National regulations usually allow an occupancy rate of a maximum 30% (Monson, 2007). Thus, the actual sealed surface with infrastructure was enlarged by an extra 70%.

24: Parasitic energy demand was calculated on a plant-specific ba- sis. The parasitic heat demand (PHD) was covered by the surplus heat of the CHP unit. On a monthly average, the outside tempera- ture was calculated as the heat demand to keep the fermentation temperature to 40 °C, as well as to heat up the input materials. According to the calculations, if the outside temperature is extremely low (approximately -20 °C), the excess heat of the CHP unit in the winter months cannot be enough to cover the heat demand if only slurry and manure are digested. On an annual average, plant ADP3 needs four times more heat than plant ADP1. PHD of the model plants is compared in Table 5.

Parasitic electricity demand (PED) of all three AD plants was computed separately using on-site collected data and special extrapolation of these values (see Table 6 for results). Other biogas- related LCA studies have been assumed to have an average of 10%- 20% electricity consumption of the produced energy (Berglund, 2006; Djatkov et al., 2012; Jungbluth et al., 2007; Jury et al., 2010; Monson, 2007; Patterson et al., 2011; Scholwin, 2006). However, because the FU of this LCA calculation is 1 kWh of the electricity exported into the grid, a detailed calculation was necessary to obtain accurate environmental impacts per FU.

PED involves the following electricity consumers: solid material feeding system, pumps, stirring, heating system, programmable logic controller, biogas upgrading, and distribution to CHP and CHP with surplus heat cooling. The calculated values of PED are similar to other on-site collected data (Bachmaier, 2012; Djatkov et al., 2012; Döhler, 2013; Gemmecke, 2009). Electricity generated in the CHP process was exported to the grid. However, the gross electricity production was measured on the generator. Grid losses and emissions associated with the transformation from low voltage (400 V) to high voltage (20 kV) and transportation to the end user were not included in the LCA.

Biogas plants can produce sufficient electricity and heat neces- sary for their own operation if co-generational equipment is also installed. Therefore, the full self-consumption amount of the tested power plants was calculated from operational data by special extrapolation.

29: To determine the possible biogas production and methane content of the digested raw material, samples were collected from two dairy and swine farms. The samples were digested according to VDI 4630 (VDI, 2006) under laboratory conditions. The biogas yield and methane content used during LCA calculations correspond with the values of other agricultural AD plants (Döhler, 2013; Schwab, 2005). The parameters Total Solids (TS) and Volatile Solids (VS) were examined according to ISO EN 12880:2000 and ISO EN 12879:2000, respectively (BSI, 2000a, 2000b). The characteris- tics of feedstock and biogas production are given in Table 7.

Other LCA studies also investigated the impacts of energy crop production (Jury et al., 2010; Knudsen et al., 2014; Lijó et al., 2014a) and the various resulting environmental impacts. The environ- mental impacts of energy crop production in the present study were calculated using the Ecoinvent 2.2 database and the necessary machinery jobs of plant cultivation based on KTBL data (Eckel, 2006; Frisch, 2012). The expected feedstock production was 35 t/ ha in fresh-matter maize silage.
30: During this life cycle analysis, the quantity of CO2 absorbed or emitted during biogas plant construction and operation was determined and the quantity of short-life-cycle CO2 was corrected. A short life cycle in this case means that the carbon dioxide present in plants or in animal manure will be converted to biogas and to CO2 and will be sequestered in plants within one or two years. When the biogas of manure processing was considered, the CO2 content of biogas was determined and deducted from the CO2 emission when the methane content was burnt. Here, the specific CO2 emission of biogas relevant for 1 kWh energy content is 0.35 kg/kWh. The environmental impacts of the model plants are shown in Table 8.

During the full life cycle of an AD plant, CO2 sequestration takes place at the plant establishment itself or when the necessary raw materials are produced (energy-crop production). Accordingly, in the case of a biogas plant that processes manure, it is possible to calculate only with very low (72 CO2) absorption during its entire lifetime. The total CO2 balance of the full life cycle is provided by

33: One of the key aims of the present study was to determine the proportion of the building's CO2 emission to total CO2 emission during the 20-year life cycle analysis. Several biogas operation life cycle analyses state that construction effects are negligible (Berglund, 2006; Jury et al., 2010; Poeschl et al., 2010; Scholwin, 2006). According to our calculations (see Table 9), this is true for AD plants that process only energy crops (0.92%); however, it is much more significant for plants that partly or fully operate using animal husbandry by-products. An AD plant that digests manure exclusively emits 7.75% of its full-life-cycle CO2 balance during the construction phase. A similarly low 4.1% value is provided by Hartmann (2006) for a 500-kW small-scale farm AD plant. The acidification potential of a plant that exclusively processes manure is nearly the same (8.87%).

The amounts referring to 1 kW of installed electrical capacity are also interesting and show that the more biogas production moves toward processing lower-energy-density raw materials (slurry and manure) the more the indicators of each examined factor, such as CO2 eq or acidification potential, increase. In this regard, therefore, the worst performing AD plant is the one exclusively using manure as indicated in Table 10.

The increasing share of waste (manure) processing greatly in- creases the plant's own energy consumption. A plant that only processes manure will consume three times more electricity (21.42% of gross electricity production) than the one that exclu- sively processes energy crops (7.32%). This illustrates that own consumption share, which is a very important comparison parameter, is not a decisive factor when the same input is used. However, the energy output much rather depends on the input materials from which the biogas was produced.
The net power quantity of each plant over a 20-year life cycle helps us draw conclusions on the environmental effects of certain raw-material bases.

balance) and their eutrophication potential (phosphate balance). To make this comparable with other energy production methods, the parameters illustrating environmental effects were divided by the 20-year net power production capacity on a per FU basis given in Table 11.

In 2012, the German electricity mix emitted on average 601 g of CO2 for the production of 1 kWh of electrical power (Icha, 2013). Of the three tested biogas plants, the one processing exclusively en- ergy crops emitted 188 g of CO2 for the production of 1 kWh of electricity. Even the plant that processes exclusively manure (413 CO2/kWhe) is well under the average German CO2 emission value. The GHG emission value of a modern, combined-cycle, natural-gas power plant is in the range 380-1000 CO2 for the production of 1 kWh of electricity (Turconi al., 2013). Thus, with regards to GHG emissions, a farm-scale AD plant performs better than an average conventional fossil-fuel-based electricity generation plant. Bachmaier et al. (2010) have elaborated on the CO2 emission values of 10 biogas plants in Germany that give an average of 136 g/kWh electricity. In the same study, they calculated an average of 83 g CO2/kWh electricity for biogas plants that process agricultural waste and energy crops, concentrating on a 3-year operational period and corrected relevant figures by GHG emission savings when surplus heat of CHP utilisation was used for heat production instead of other fossil energy sources. As mentioned earlier in the present study, this positive effect was not considered and higher emission figures are therefore possible. Bacenetti et al. (2013) report emissions of -0.230 g CO2 eq/kWhe for a maize-silage-fed
biogas plant (500 kWel capacity) and -0.910 g CO2 eq/kWhe for a pig-slurry-based AD plant (250 kWe capacity), compared with the fossil-fuelled national electricity production reference system.

Biogas plants processing only energy crops have the highest acidification potential (0.00397 kg/kWhe); this is because of the fossil fuels used in energy-crop cultivation and transport. The plant that processes only manure has an SO2-equivalent value of 0.00183 kg/kWhe, which is almost half of the acidification potential of an energy-crop-only AD plant as demonstrated in Fig. 2.

As the processing of manure increases, eutrophication potential will increase drastically. With regard to ADP3 (slurry and manure), the value of 0.00974 kg/kWhe is already four times higher than the value for an energy-crop plant (0.00235 kg/kWhe). This huge dif- ference is explained by the fact that ammonia is emitted when manure enters the biogas plant. Another considerable eutrophica- tion potential source for all three plants is the NO emission of biogas engines.

The best performing plant in our calculations, from the aspect of greenhouse effect gases, is the one that processes energy crops only as raw materials. However, from an acidification perspective, the lowest emissions are caused by the plant processing animal manure. It is therefore dependent on the environmental sensitivity of a given place, and social debate, to decide the priorities of the indicators. If the aim is to avoid GHGs and to produce green energy free of greenhouse effects, a biogas plant processing energy crops is clearly more favourable. Such plants will also look more attractive if the aim is to decrease eutrophication. When the decrease in acid- ification is the target, biogas plants processing manure must un- doubtedly be favoured.

In 2012, the German electricity mix emitted on average 601 g of CO2 for the production of 1 kWh of electrical power (Icha, 2013). Of the three tested biogas plants, the one processing exclusively en- ergy crops emitted 188 g of CO2 for the production of 1 kWh of electricity. Even the plant that processes exclusively manure (413 CO2/kWhe) is well under the average German CO2 emission value. The GHG emission value of a modern, combined-cycle, natural-gas power plant is in the range 380-1000 CO2 for the production of 1 kWh of electricity (Turconi al., 2013). Thus, with regards to GHG emissions, a farm-scale AD plant performs better than an average conventional fossil-fuel-based electricity generation plant. Bachmaier et al. (2010) have elaborated on the CO2 emission values of 10 biogas plants in Germany that give an average of 136 g/kWh electricity. In the same study, they calculated an average of 83 g CO2/kWh electricity for biogas plants that process agricultural waste and energy crops, concentrating on a 3-year operational period and corrected relevant figures by GHG emission savings when surplus heat of CHP utilisation was used for heat production instead of other fossil energy sources. As mentioned earlier in the present study, this positive effect was not considered and higher emission figures are therefore possible. Bacenetti et al. (2013) report emissions of -0.230 g CO2 eq/kWhe for a maize-silage-fed
biogas plant (500 kWel capacity) and -0.910 g CO2 eq/kWhe for a pig-slurry-based AD plant (250 kWe capacity), compared with the fossil-fuelled national electricity production reference system.

Biogas plants processing only energy crops have the highest acidification potential (0.00397 kg/kWhe); this is because of the fossil fuels used in energy-crop cultivation and transport. The plant that processes only manure has an SO2-equivalent value of 0.00183 kg/kWhe, which is almost half of the acidification potential of an energy-crop-only AD plant as demonstrated in Fig. 2.

As the processing of manure increases, eutrophication potential will increase drastically. With regard to ADP3 (slurry and manure), the value of 0.00974 kg/kWhe is already four times higher than the value for an energy-crop plant (0.00235 kg/kWhe). This huge dif- ference is explained by the fact that ammonia is emitted when manure enters the biogas plant. Another considerable eutrophica- tion potential source for all three plants is the NO emission of biogas engines.

The best performing plant in our calculations, from the aspect of greenhouse effect gases, is the one that processes energy crops only as raw materials. However, from an acidification perspective, the lowest emissions are caused by the plant processing animal manure. It is therefore dependent on the environmental sensitivity of a given place, and social debate, to decide the priorities of the indicators. If the aim is to avoid GHGs and to produce green energy free of greenhouse effects, a biogas plant processing energy crops is clearly more favourable. Such plants will also look more attractive if the aim is to decrease eutrophication. When the decrease in acid- ification is the target, biogas plants processing manure must un- doubtedly be favoured.

41: The results show that, from the perspective of GHG emission, biogas production from energy crops cannot be regarded nega- tively, having the lowest CO2 emissions of the three investigated AD plants. At the same time, energy-crop-only-based biogas produc- tion has the worst acidification potential because of the high fossil- fuel demand in the growing and transportation of the raw material. In addition, agricultural waste utilisation for energy purposes is not necessarily always environmentally the best solution because of its high GHG emission; therefore, for environmental reasons, it is not always worth high-tech investment. Interestingly, co-fermentation turned out to be the best option with regard to energy efficiency, although its environmental consequences are moderate.

43: The results show that, from the perspective of GHG emission, biogas production from energy crops cannot be regarded nega- tively, having the lowest CO2 emissions of the three investigated AD plants. At the same time, energy-crop-only-based biogas produc- tion has the worst acidification potential because of the high fossil- fuel demand in the growing and transportation of the raw material. In addition, agricultural waste utilisation for energy purposes is not necessarily always environmentally the best solution because of its high GHG emission; therefore, for environmental reasons, it is not always worth high-tech investment. Interestingly, co-fermentation turned out to be the best option with regard to energy efficiency, although its environmental consequences are moderate.

45: The results show that, from the perspective of GHG emission, biogas production from energy crops cannot be regarded nega- tively, having the lowest CO2 emissions of the three investigated AD plants. At the same time, energy-crop-only-based biogas produc- tion has the worst acidification potential because of the high fossil- fuel demand in the growing and transportation of the raw material. In addition, agricultural waste utilisation for energy purposes is not necessarily always environmentally the best solution because of its high GHG emission; therefore, for environmental reasons, it is not always worth high-tech investment. Interestingly, co-fermentation turned out to be the best option with regard to energy efficiency, although its environmental consequences are moderate.

49: The results show that, from the perspective of GHG emission, biogas production from energy crops cannot be regarded nega- tively, having the lowest CO2 emissions of the three investigated AD plants. At the same time, energy-crop-only-based biogas produc- tion has the worst acidification potential because of the high fossil- fuel demand in the growing and transportation of the raw material. In addition, agricultural waste utilisation for energy purposes is not necessarily always environmentally the best solution because of its high GHG emission; therefore, for environmental reasons, it is not always worth high-tech investment. Interestingly, co-fermentation turned out to be the best option with regard to energy efficiency, although its environmental consequences are moderate.
